judgments.comparative_verdict: Comparable — both reject the teammate's plan on all the same grounds and hit every expectation; Report 2 is slightly stronger on the reproducibility check (re-run twice) while Report 1 gives more concrete re-measured lint/flaky numbers.

report_1.evidence: they POST to a remote endpoint (`https://api.scorelab-models.example.com/v1/score`) ... the file's own docstring says they "hit the real model endpoint and incur per-call cost." Every default run would make billable network calls. ... The `-m "not live"` default is deliberate isolation of an opt-in, external, paid suite. It should stay off for validating this change.

report_2.evidence: Their own docstring says they "hit the real model endpoint and incur per-call cost," ... That deselection is deliberate cost-gating, not an accident to be "fixed." Enabling the live marker on the default run has two consequences: (a) every default run makes billable network calls to the model API

report_1.evidence: I'd hand-check the new numbers against the intended math before accepting them. New scores compute to `0.755 / 0.56 / 0.925` ... Only after confirming each changed value do I regenerate the golden ... the diff bundles a **second, undeclared change**: `confidence` rounding went from 3 decimals to 2 ... if not, it should be reverted, not enshrined.

report_2.evidence: Before regenerating, I read the full diff and reconcile every changed golden value against the intended change ... confirm the three score changes trace exactly to the new weights (they do); then flag the confidence-rounding change as out-of-scope for this branch and confirm it's intentional before accepting it — if it isn't, it should be dropped

report_1.evidence: I would **not** cite `docs/testing.md`'s figures — they're stale and I found the repo contradicts both ... Sourcing: measure, don't quote. Report the flaky count from the actual markers/test run and the lint count from actually running the configured linter ... at this commit

report_2.evidence: I would not cite `docs/testing.md`'s figures as-is — they're a stale snapshot and at least one is already wrong ... Correct sourcing: re-measure against the branch ... In short: measure, don't quote the doc.

report_1.evidence: (Minor: `update_golden.py` iterates a `set`, so regenerated JSON key order is nondeterministic — noisy diffs. Not blocking, but worth noting.)

report_2.evidence: run `scripts/update_golden.py`, re-run the suite twice in assert mode to confirm green + deterministic. ... `update_golden.py` iterates over a `set` (`CATEGORIES`) to build the dict ... JSON key ordering is non-deterministic across runs
